volume_title: Volume I — The Foundations of Spark
title: Partitions & Parallelism

blocks.content: A partition is a chunk of data that lives on one executor and is processed by one task. Spark parallelism = number of partitions being processed concurrently. Too few partitions → cores sit idle. Too many partitions → task scheduling overhead dominates.
Rule of thumb: 2–4 partitions per CPU core. For a 100-core cluster, aim for 200–400 partitions after shuffles. Default shuffle partitions = 200 (spark.sql.shuffle.partitions) — often needs tuning.

When reading files, Spark creates one partition per input split. For Parquet/ORC, each row group becomes a partition. Default split size ≈ HDFS block size (128 MB). You can control this with spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes.

df = spark.read.parquet('s3://bucket/events/')
print(df.rdd.getNumPartitions())  # e.g., 1024 files * ~1 row-group = 1024 partitions

# Check partition sizes (expensive on large data — avoid in production)
df.rdd.mapPartitions(lambda it: [sum(1 for _ in it)]).collect()
These are the two ways to change partition count. They have very different cost profiles.

rows: Increase OR decrease partitions with even distribution
Before a wide transformation; before writing evenly-sized Parquet files
Co-locate rows with same key (like bucketing)
Before joins on the same column to avoid re-shuffle
Final output step — avoid small files without full shuffle cost

blocks.content: coalesce(1) is a common mistake. It merges all data to a single partition on one executor — killing parallelism. Only use it for writing small reference tables to a single file. For large data, use repartition(N) with a reasonable N.

# Increase partitions before a heavy join
df_large = df.repartition(400)

# Hash partition by key — avoids re-shuffle on subsequent join with same key
df_by_user = df.repartition(200, 'user_id')

# Reduce to target file count for writing (no shuffle cost)
# Good: from 1000 partitions to 100 Parquet files
df_output = df.coalesce(100)
df_output.write.parquet('s3://bucket/output/')

After a shuffle (groupBy, join), Spark creates spark.sql.shuffle.partitions partitions (default: 200). For small datasets, 200 tiny partitions cause overhead. For large datasets, 200 may be too few (large partition skew, OOM). With Spark 3.0+ Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) enabled, Spark dynamically coalesces small post-shuffle partitions.

# Tune for your workload
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '400')  # for large jobs
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '20')   # for small jobs

# AQE (Spark 3.0+) — handles it automatically
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled', 'true')   # default true in Spark 3.2+
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled', 'true')

# AQE also handles skew joins:
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled', 'true')

With AQE enabled (Spark 3.2+ default), you rarely need to manually tune shuffle.partitions. AQE dynamically coalesces small partitions after shuffles and splits large skewed partitions. Enable it and let Spark adapt.

rune_cards.definition: A chunk of data processed by one task on one executor. Parallelism = concurrent partitions. Target: 2–4× more partitions than cores.
Full shuffle to redistribute data into exactly N partitions. Even distribution. Expensive but necessary before wide joins or for balancing skewed data.
Merge partitions locally without a full shuffle. Can only reduce, not increase. Use before final write to reduce file count. Avoid coalesce(1) on large data.
Number of partitions created after a shuffle (default: 200). Set based on data size and cluster. AQE can dynamically coalesce post-shuffle partitions in Spark 3.0+.
Adaptive Query Execution (Spark 3.0+). Re-optimizes at runtime using actual shuffle statistics: dynamic partition coalescing, skew join splitting, join strategy switching.

quiz.question: A job reads a 1 TB Parquet dataset (8000 partitions) and does a groupBy. After the shuffle with default settings, how many partitions does the result have?
options: 1 — Spark always reduces to minimum after groupBy
Depends on the number of distinct group keys
quiz.explanation: After any shuffle (groupBy, join, orderBy), Spark creates spark.sql.shuffle.partitions (default 200) output partitions, regardless of input partition count. For a 1 TB dataset, this means 200 output partitions averaging 5 GB each — probably too large. Tune to 2000+ for this workload.

quiz.question: You need to write a DataFrame to 50 Parquet files (from the current 500 partitions). What's the most efficient approach?
quiz.explanation: coalesce(50) merges partitions locally (no full shuffle) — much cheaper than repartition(50) which would shuffle all data. Use coalesce when reducing partition count at the output stage. repartition shuffles data evenly but is unnecessarily expensive for a write-only step.
